meta.title: Django
meta.description: Django web applications, ORM, views, middleware, and template security

items.text: Are model field types and constraints appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Model fields should use the most appropriate type (CharField vs TextField, DecimalField vs FloatField). Constraints like max_length, unique, and blank/null should be set correctly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong field types cause data integrity issues. Using FloatField for money loses precision. Missing constraints allow invalid data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review each field type against the data it stores. Check that null=True and blank=True are used intentionally, not as defaults.
baseContent.exampleComment: price uses FloatField which has precision issues. Use DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2) for monetary values.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Choose field types deliberately. Understand the difference between null and blank.

items.text: Are migrations correct, reversible, and safe for production?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Django migrations should be reviewed for correctness. RunPython operations need reverse functions. Large table alterations may need special handling.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broken migrations can corrupt data or cause downtime. Irreversible migrations prevent rollbacks during failed deployments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that RunPython has a reverse_code. Verify AddField on large tables won't lock them. Test with --fake-initial if needed.
baseContent.exampleComment: This RunPython migration has no reverse_code. Add a reverse function or use migrations.RunPython.noop if truly irreversible.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always provide reverse migrations. Test migrations against production-scale data.

items.text: Are QuerySet operations efficient (no N+1, proper select_related/prefetch_related)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Accessing related objects without select_related (ForeignKey) or prefetch_related (ManyToMany/reverse FK) causes N+1 queries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: N+1 queries are the #1 Django performance issue. Listing 100 items with related objects makes 101 queries instead of 2.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check templates and serializers for related object access. Use django-debug-toolbar or django-silk to count queries.
baseContent.exampleComment: This view accesses post.author in a loop but doesn't use select_related('author'). Add it to the queryset.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use select_related for ForeignKey, prefetch_related for ManyToMany. Always check query counts.

items.text: Are database indexes defined for frequently queried fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Fields used in filter(), order_by(), or lookups should have db_index=True or be included in Meta.indexes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing indexes cause full table scans that get slower as data grows. A query filtering by status on 1M rows without an index is very slow.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check filter() and order_by() calls. Verify the filtered fields have indexes. Use Meta.indexes for composite indexes.
baseContent.exampleComment: This view filters by status and created_at but neither field is indexed. Add a composite index in Meta.indexes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Index fields you filter and sort by. Use composite indexes for multi-field queries.

items.text: Are custom managers and querysets used for reusable query logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Repeated query patterns should be encapsulated in custom managers or QuerySet methods rather than duplicated across views.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated query logic is error-prone. Changing the logic requires finding and updating every copy.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for repeated filter chains across views. Check if they could be extracted to a custom manager or queryset method.
baseContent.exampleComment: The active/published filter is repeated in 4 views. Consider a PublishedManager with get_queryset filtering by is_published=True.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Encapsulate reusable query patterns in managers. Keep views focused on request/response logic.

items.text: Is the choice between class-based and function-based views appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CBVs are good for standard CRUD. FBVs are better for complex custom logic. The choice should be consistent within the project.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using CBVs for simple custom logic leads to overriding too many methods. Using FBVs for CRUD leads to boilerplate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that CBVs aren't overriding most methods (sign to use FBV). Check that FBVs doing CRUD aren't reimplementing what a CBV provides.
baseContent.exampleComment: This CBV overrides get, post, get_context_data, and form_valid. A function-based view would be clearer here.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use CBVs for standard patterns, FBVs for custom logic. Be consistent within the project.

items.text: Are views protected with appropriate permission checks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Views should check authentication and authorization. Use @login_required, @permission_required, or LoginRequiredMixin/PermissionRequiredMixin.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing permission checks allow unauthorized access to data and actions. This is a critical security vulnerability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that views handling sensitive data have permission decorators or mixins. Verify object-level permissions for owner-only access.
baseContent.exampleComment: This view shows user profile data but has no @login_required. Any anonymous user can access other users' profiles.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always add permission checks. Use object-level permissions for owner-specific resources.

items.text: Are forms properly validated and errors displayed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Django forms should be used for input validation. Errors should be displayed to users. Never trust request.POST directly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Skipping form validation allows invalid or malicious data into the database. Missing error display frustrates users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that views use form.is_valid() before processing. Verify form errors are rendered in templates.
baseContent.exampleComment: This view accesses request.POST['email'] directly without form validation. Use a Django form to validate the input.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always validate input through Django forms. Display validation errors clearly to users.

items.text: Do views handle HTTP methods correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Views should respond to appropriate HTTP methods. State changes should use POST/PUT/DELETE, not GET. Use @require_http_methods or allowed_methods.
baseContent.whyItMatters: GET requests for state changes are vulnerable to CSRF (via img tags, links) and can be triggered by crawlers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for views that modify data on GET requests. Verify function-based views restrict to appropriate methods.
baseContent.exampleComment: This view deletes a record on GET request. Use POST or DELETE with @require_POST decorator.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never modify state on GET. Use @require_POST or @require_http_methods for mutation views.

items.text: Are list views paginated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Views that list objects should use Django's Paginator or ListView's paginate_by to limit results per page.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unpaginated lists load all objects into memory, causing OOM errors and slow response times as data grows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check list views for pagination. Verify paginate_by is set on ListViews. Check that templates include pagination controls.
baseContent.exampleComment: This ListView has no paginate_by. With 100K records, this will be extremely slow. Add paginate_by = 25.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always paginate list views. Set a reasonable page size based on your UI.

items.text: Is CSRF protection enabled and properly used?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Django's CSRF middleware should be enabled. Forms must include {% csrf_token %}. AJAX requests need the CSRF cookie header.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Disabling CSRF protection or using @csrf_exempt allows cross-site request forgery attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for @csrf_exempt usage. Verify forms include {% csrf_token %}. Check that AJAX setups include CSRF headers.
baseContent.exampleComment: This view uses @csrf_exempt without justification. Remove it or add a comment explaining why CSRF protection is disabled.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never disable CSRF without a documented reason. Always include {% csrf_token %} in forms.

items.text: Is raw SQL avoided or properly parameterized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Django's ORM prevents SQL injection by default. Raw SQL (raw(), extra(), RawSQL) must use parameterized queries, never string formatting.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection via raw queries can lead to complete database compromise, data theft, and data destruction.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for .raw(), .extra(), RawSQL, and cursor.execute(). Verify they use parameterized queries with %s placeholders.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses f-string formatting in raw(): Model.objects.raw(f'SELECT * WHERE id={id}'). Use parameterized: raw('SELECT * WHERE id=%s', [id]).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the ORM whenever possible. When raw SQL is needed, always parameterize.

items.text: Is template auto-escaping preserved (no unsafe |safe or {% autoescape off %})?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Django auto-escapes template variables to prevent XSS. Using |safe or {% autoescape off %} disables this protection.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Marking user input as safe enables XSS attacks. Attackers can inject JavaScript that steals sessions and credentials.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for |safe and {% autoescape off %}. Verify the content is truly trusted (not user-generated) before marking safe.
baseContent.exampleComment: This renders user.bio|safe. If users can edit their bio, this is an XSS vulnerability. Remove |safe or sanitize the HTML.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never mark user input as safe. Use bleach or similar to sanitize HTML if needed.

items.text: Are SECRET_KEY and credentials kept out of settings.py?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SECRET_KEY, database passwords, and API keys should be loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager, not hardcoded in settings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded secrets in source code are exposed to everyone with repository access. Leaked SECRET_KEY compromises all sessions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check settings.py for hardcoded credentials. Verify SECRET_KEY, DATABASE_PASSWORD, and API keys use os.environ or django-environ.
baseContent.exampleComment: SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in settings.py. Load it from an environment variable: SECRET_KEY = os.environ['DJANGO_SECRET_KEY'].
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never hardcode secrets. Use environment variables or a secrets manager.

items.text: Is DEBUG=False in production settings?

baseContent.whatItMeans: DEBUG=True exposes detailed error pages with stack traces, settings, and environment variables to users.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Debug pages leak sensitive information: database credentials, API keys, file paths, and installed packages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check production settings for DEBUG=True. Verify ALLOWED_HOSTS is set (required when DEBUG=False).
baseContent.exampleComment: DEBUG is set to True in the production settings file. Set it to False and configure ALLOWED_HOSTS.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never run DEBUG=True in production. Use separate settings files for development and production.

items.text: Do serializers validate all input fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: DRF serializers should validate input using field-level validators, validate() method, or Meta.extra_kwargs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing validation allows invalid data into the database. Over-permissive serializers may allow mass-assignment attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that serializers define fields explicitly (not fields = '__all__'). Verify read_only_fields excludes sensitive fields.
baseContent.exampleComment: This serializer uses fields = '__all__' which exposes is_staff and is_superuser. List fields explicitly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always list serializer fields explicitly. Use read_only_fields for fields users shouldn't modify.

items.text: Are DRF permission classes properly configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ViewSets and APIViews should define permission_classes. Use IsAuthenticated, IsAdminUser, or custom permissions as appropriate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The default permission is AllowAny in many configs. Missing permission classes expose endpoints to unauthenticated users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that sensitive ViewSets have permission_classes. Verify DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES in settings is restrictive.
baseContent.exampleComment: This ViewSet has no permission_classes and the default is AllowAny. Add permission_classes = [IsAuthenticated].
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set restrictive default permissions. Override per-view only when needed.

items.text: Is API rate limiting configured?

baseContent.whatItMeans: DRF provides throttling classes to limit request rates. Configure DEFAULT_THROTTLE_RATES for anonymous and authenticated users.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting, APIs are vulnerable to abuse, brute force attacks, and resource exhaustion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check settings for DEFAULT_THROTTLE_CLASSES and DEFAULT_THROTTLE_RATES. Verify sensitive endpoints have tighter limits.
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no throttling configured. Add DEFAULT_THROTTLE_RATES: {'anon': '100/hour', 'user': '1000/hour'} to settings.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Configure rate limiting for all APIs. Use tighter limits for authentication endpoints.

items.text: Are views and models tested with meaningful test cases?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Views should be tested for correct status codes, template usage, and context. Models should test custom methods and constraints.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested views can break silently. Missing model tests allow business logic regressions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for test files matching the views and models being changed. Verify tests cover success, failure, and edge cases.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new view has no tests. Add tests for authenticated/unauthenticated access, valid/invalid input, and the success case.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test views for access control, input validation, and correct responses. Test models for business logic.

items.text: Do tests use Django's test client and RequestFactory appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use TestCase with self.client for integration tests. Use RequestFactory for unit-testing individual views without middleware.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using the wrong test tool makes tests either too slow (full client for unit tests) or too shallow (missing middleware effects).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that integration tests use the test client (tests middleware, URL routing). Check that unit tests use RequestFactory when appropriate.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test creates a Request manually. Use self.client.get() to test through the full middleware stack.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the test client for full integration tests. Use RequestFactory when you need to test a view function in isolation.

items.text: Are test fixtures manageable and not overly coupled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use factory_boy or model_bakery instead of large JSON fixtures. Create only the data each test needs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large shared fixtures are fragile, slow to load, and hard to maintain. Changes to one fixture can break unrelated tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for large fixture files. Verify tests create their own data or use factories rather than depending on shared state.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test depends on a 500-line fixture file. Use factory_boy to create only the objects this test needs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use factories over fixtures. Each test should create its own minimal data.
